weather: Today in Edmonds: abundant sunshine, high 63°F with light northerly winds 5-10 mph. Clear skies overnight, low 47°F. No precipitation expected—perfect for outdoor activities with the family. Dress in layers as mornings start cool but warm nicely by midday.

local_news.findings: The Edmonds City Council has approved key contracts and budget amendments during its April 7 business meeting. Notable approvals include a $415,116 contract with Jacobs Engineering for wastewater treatment plant gasification system support, a $419,000 sewer rehabilitation project covering 27 locations, and personnel reclassifications including upgrading the HR assistant position to HR Analyst. The Council also declined to pursue a counterpetition in the Redelfs tree litigation case, where a 33-inch landmark tree was removed without permit, resulting in a reduced fine from $102,245 to $20,000.

Edmonds has approved a $415,000 contract with Jacobs Engineering to finally operate its long-delayed gasification system at the wastewater treatment plant. After three years of technical issues since initial attempts in April 2023, including equipment failures and system clogs, the city hopes to begin consistent operations this month. The gasification technology, provided by Ecoremedy, converts human waste into dried solids and was the first in the nation to destroy PFAS "forever chemicals" in biosolids.

South County Fire commissioners are working on redistricting proposals to incorporate Edmonds' 42,000+ residents into the Regional Fire Authority's five commissioner districts. Two plans have been presented: Plan 1 would place nearly all of Edmonds into a single district (District 2), while Plan 2 would split the city at Pine Street between Districts 1 and 2. The board will hold public hearings to gather community input before finalizing district boundaries that must comply with RCW 29A.76.010 requirements for equal population, compactness, and community preservation.

The Washington State Court of Appeals has reopened the Edmonds tree mandate case involving resident Nathan Rimmer, who sued the city over unconstitutional tree replacement requirements for his home construction permit. The court had previously dismissed the case as moot after Rimmer sold the property, but his attorney filed a motion for reconsideration arguing the city should still pay damages despite the property sale. The city has 15 days to respond to the motion.

intellectual_journals.findings: A sweeping ProPublica investigation reveals how the Trump administration has systematically dismantled federal safeguards meant to protect elections. The report documents at least 75 career staff departures across agencies, replaced by two dozen appointees from the election denial movement—ten of whom directly participated in efforts to overturn the 2020 vote. The piece reconstructs a previously unreported December 2020 meeting where Attorney General William Barr interrogated CISA experts about hacked voting machines, ultimately accepting his resignation rather than endorse false fraud claims. [TRUNCATED]

A new Aeon essay examines how 13th-century mosaics in Venice's Saint Mark's Basilica transformed the human body into a theological-political instrument. The Zen Chapel's gold mosaics depict Saint Mark's martyrdom with deliberate visual tension—while executioners strain and heave, the saint remains composed, his silence signifying spiritual mastery over physical pain. The essay argues medieval artists used such imagery to naturalize social hierarchies, making political roles appear as inherent bodily qualities. A striking Black executioner figure carries out violence, embodying how medieval art [TRUNCATED]

The Marginalian profiles Frances 'Fanny' Wright, the forgotten 19th-century Scottish radical who pioneered scientific thinking and free love in America. Born in 1795 and orphaned as a toddler, Wright became a towering intellectual figure who inspired Walt Whitman's democratic vision through her radical paper The Free Enquirer. The essay traces her journey from studying Epicurean philosophy at Glasgow University to becoming America's first female public lecturer, advocating for abolition, women's rights, and experimental communes. Her 1829 Nashoba community in Tennessee attempted interracial ed [TRUNCATED]

Francine Prose reviews two contemporary novels—Lee Clay Johnson's Bloodline and Carl Hiaasen's Fever Beach—that tackle America's current political violence through dark satire. Johnson's Bloodline follows a delusional former car salesman who amputates his own hand in an insurance scam, believing himself descended from Confederate General John Hunt Morgan. The novel explores how historical fantasies fuel present-day grievances, as the protagonist renames himself 'Wins-a-ton' and attempts to reclaim his imagined birthright through increasingly unhinged schemes. Hiaasen's Fever Beach similarly us [TRUNCATED]

The Denver-based platform now employs 224 people (+138% YoY) and offers comprehensive AI tools for lesson planning, assessments, IEPs, and differentiation. Diffit for Teachers (Berkeley-based) has grown to 20 employees (+39%  [TRUNCATED]

The wearable AI landscape has expanded beyond pendants to include smart glasses and lifelogging devices. Halliday offers proactive AI glasses with invisible display technology, while WARE AI (formerly Titan) provides Series 100 wearables with camera/audio capabilities. Compass fo [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Three recent papers advance triadic, relational ontologies. Christopher Isabelle’s “Triadic Relational Ontology” argues that dyadic distinctions (self/other, here/not-here) are insufficient for intelligibility; a third “relational field” must be actively sustained. Damiano Migliorini’s 2025 monograph integrates trinitarian metaphysics with analytic relational ontology, proposing a non-substantialist model of divine personhood. Kenneth Hammat’s February 2026 “Triadic Neurophenomenology” unifies neuroscience and phenomenology around three irreducible domains—object (material), idea (mental), and relation (axiological)—offering testable hypotheses for consciousness and AI.

ai_systems.findings: Three landmark 2026 papers push autonomous research systems beyond the hype cycle. InternAgent-1.5 (arXiv:2602.08990) introduces a unified three-subsystem architecture—generation, verification, evolution—that coordinates computational modeling and wet-lab experimentation within a single agent, achieving state-of-the-art scores on GAIA, HLE and FrontierScience benchmarks while autonomously designing competitive ML algorithms. Google’s MARS framework (arXiv:2602.02660) tackles the cost-credit problem in AI research with budget-aware Monte-Carlo planning and reflective memory, delivering SOTA on MLE-Bench and exhibiting spontaneous “Aha!” cross-branch transfer in 63 % of learned lessons. The Mimosa system (arXiv:2603.28986) breaks fixed-workflow limits by synthesizing task-specific multi-agent topologies on the fly, using the new Model Context Protocol for dynamic tool discovery and reaching 43.1 % success on ScienceAgentBench—showing that workflow evolution must be matched to model capab [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congressional UAP disclosure has reached unprecedented intensity in 2026. The House Oversight Task Force, led by Rep. Anna Paulina Luna, continues aggressive transparency efforts, demanding 46 classified UAP videos from the Pentagon by April 14 deadline, with subpoena threats pending. President Trump announced "very interesting documents" will be released "very, very soon" following his February directive to declassify UFO files. Defense Secretary Hegseth confirms Pentagon's All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) is coordinating with White House to release "never-before-seen" UAP information, while AARO's caseload has ballooned to over 2,000 cases. Recent congressional hearings revealed explosive testimony: former intelligence officer David Grusch confirmed recovery of "non-human biologics" from crash sites, while whistleblower Dylan Borland described witnessing a 100-foot triangular craft at Langley AFB with impossible physics. Rep. Tim Burchett cited classified Navy reports of f [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: The Talk of the Town: April 27, 2026
newyorker.dek: Notes on the White House Correspondents’ Dinner, the future of the New Yorker, and the return of the cicadas.
newyorker.text:
The White House Correspondents’ Dinner, held last Saturday night, was a surreal affair. President Trump, fresh from his victory lap after the failed assassination attempt, gave a rambling speech that veered from self-congratulation to attacks on the press. The room was tense, with journalists unsure whether to laugh or look away. The New Yorker’s own David Remnick, who had been seated at the head table, later told me that he had never seen anything quite like it. “It was like watching a stand-up routine where the punchlines were indictments,” he said.

Meanwhile, the cicadas have returned to Washington, D.C., after seventeen years underground. The sound is deafening, a constant hum that seems to echo the city’s own nervous energy. At the New Yorker offices, we’ve been debating whether to dedicate a special issue to the insects, or perhaps to the broader theme of emergence—of things long buried coming to light. The cicadas, after all, are a reminder that some cycles are inevitable, even in a city that prides itself on breaking them.

In other news, the magazine’s art department has unveiled a new cover design, a minimalist take on the Washington Monument that doubles as a cicada wing. It’s a subtle nod to the city’s dual nature: power and fragility, permanence and change. The design has already sparked debate on social media, with some praising its elegance and others calling it too obscure. But then, obscurity has always been part of the New Yorker’s charm.
